titleStmt.title: Geata an Phóirtéaraigh | Portersgate

editor.orgName: Maynooth University, Department of Early Irish
Maynooth University, Department of Early Irish

titleStmt.funder: Irish Research Council (grant no. IRC/W001985/1) and Arts and Humanities Research Council (grant no. AH/W001985/1)

funder.ref: UK-Ireland Collaboration in the Digital Humanities Research Grant

respStmt.name: David Stifter
respStmt.orgName: Maynooth University, Department of Early Irish

respStmt.name: Robert Shaw
respStmt.resp: 3D laser scanned using Artec Eva scanner and later processed using Geomagic software

msItem.textLang: Primitive Irish written in ogham script

support.p: National Monuments Service SMR ID: WX054-004----

supportDesc.condition: Three fragments of this reddish, medium-grained sandstone pillar survive. As pointed out by Macalister (1945, 50-51), the break between the two larger fragments must have been of long standing as the broken surfaces are weathered and waterworn. The third, smaller fragment shows less signs of weathering and may have been buried for some time.

layoutDesc.layout: The inscription is on the rounded sides running up-top-down. It begins and ends at roughly the same position on each side, approximately a third of the way up the stone.

handDesc.handNote: The ogham strokes appear to have been v-cut with a flat chisel and are generally clear and evenly spaced without word division.

origin.origPlace: Portersgate (Geata an Phóirtéaraigh), Co. Wexford, Ireland 52.1514, -6.8894
origin.origDate: late sixth or early seventh century AD

history.provenance: The lower (and largest) fragment of the stone was discovered beneath the clay cliff under the ruins of St. Bricane's Church in 1845 by Mr Hugh Nevin of Waterford in the course of some geological researches on the promontory of Hook (Graves and Nevin 1854, 179). The second fragment (most of the top) was discovered almost 100 years later near the church by Rev. Thos. Cloney, P.P., Templetown, Fethard (Macalister 1930, 54). The third small fragment was recovered in the 1987 excavation (Breen 1988, 30-31). Although Macalister (1945, 50) names this stone 'Houseland', the find site is actually in the townland of Portersgate. The (ruined remains of the) early church (WX054-003002-) known as St. Brecaun’s church is situated at the edge of a low clay cliff (H c. 5m) on the E side of the low-lying and narrow part of the Hook peninsula. The graveyard was regarded as eroded away long ago by the sea (Redmond 1898, 32-33), and archaeological excavation in 1987 found [fragments of human bone but] no burials anywhere. However, evidence of an enclosing ditch feature is confirmed in a fosse (Wth c. 2m) partly excavated c. 12m WSW of the church (Breen 1988). This feature (Wth c. 3m; D c. 0.8m) can also be seen in the cliff-face c. 25m N of the church (Archaeological Survey of Ireland, Field Report 2017). Most of the church has now also been lost through coastal erosion.
National Museum of Ireland (seen and recorded in 3D in Jan 2015).

listChange.change: Jef Bucas autogenerated EpiDoc output from spreadsheet
Added and encoded further data, including edition
Encoded name and date information within provenance
Reverted change list order, and added Macalister sketch
Corrected image path and added Text Class referencing FAIR vocabularies for epigraphy
Added idno element for SMR number, deleted titles in edition, deleted free text in bibl
Added Irish placename to title, proofreading updates, moved logainm reference to around Irish placename, added citation details to image description.
Updated execution encoding key from v-cut to chiselled
Updated edition and removed extra lbs as continuous text. Added app (from condition) and linguistic commentary from DS (and added DS to respStmt)

media.desc: 3d model by Discovery Programme on SketchFab

app.note: A large part of the pillar, containing the end of the first name, the formula word MAQQI and the beginning of the second name, is missing.Macalister (1945, 50-51) noted that the surviving inscription is quite legible except the R, where the fifth stroke and part of the fourth are lost at the break in the stone, and the following C, which is spalled away except the distal ends. Based on the space available and what might be reasonably expected, Macalister (1945, 50, see illustration) suggested a restoration of the inscription, adopted in this edition. The third fragment discovered in 1987 is too small to confirm Macalister's restoration but, with four identifiable scores and the remains of a probable fifth, it could be a Q of MAQQI.

div.p: of Sétnae (*Sēddani̯as) son of Cathub (*Catubuts) descendant of Dercmasach (*Dercamussācas)

SEDAN[I] corresponds to OIr. Sétnae < *sentani̯o- or *santani̯o-. The name is attested possibly two more times as S[E]Ḍ[A]NI in I-KER-062 (= CIIC 194; Rathduff 1, Co. Kerry) and as SEDDOINI in I-KER-004 (= CIIC 137; Fortwilliam, Co. Kerry). The name could be related with the Gaulish ethnonym Sentones, but the suffix does not match exactly. Etymologically, SEDA­N[I] belongs either with OIr. ¹sét ‘path, way’ < *sentu- (cf. W hynt, Corn. hins, Bret. hent, Gaul. sento-) or, perhaps, with OIr. ²sét ‘object of value’. The viability of the second alternative depends on its etymology.

CATTA­BBOTT corresponds to the Early OIr. tt-stem name nom. Cathub, gen. Cathbad, which was later re-made into the o-stem nom. Cathbad, gen. Cathbaid. It is a compound of *katu- ‘battle’ + the verbal root *but- ‘to hit’ that is found in the OIr. verb fo·botha ‘to scare, alarm, frighten’ < *ṷo-but-ā-. The name is also attested in the more conservative spelling CATTUBUTTAS in I-COR-004 (= CIIC 58: Greenhill 2, Co. Cork). The replacement by -A- of the thematic vowel *-u- of *katu- is probably due to its phonetic weakening in unstressed position (McManus 1991: 118); the pheno­me­non has a parallel in CATABAR (I-WAT-045 = CIIC 303; Ballyquin, Co. Waterford) < *katu-barro- ‘battle-top, helmet’. It may also be connected with the inflection of compound names with cath as their second element as o-stems instead of the expected u-stems, as evidenced by [CAT]TABBOTT in the same inscription.

AVVI DERCMASOC ‘descendant of Dercmassach’ appears to be the Uí Dercmossaig kin group. However, according to later genealogical sources, this group were located further North in the Dublin area. The analysis of DERCMA­SOC is ambiguous. Taken at face value, it is a com­pound of two words, DERC and MASOC. Identifying the words is difficult. DERC appears to correspond either to OIr. ¹derc ‘hole’ (dil.ie/15580) or to ²derc ‘eye’ (dil.ie/15581; the two may in fact have been a single word ori­ginally). Alternatively, if the inscription is so late that OIr. spelling practices made themselves already felt, DERC could stand for derg ‘red’, with the letter C representing the sound /g/ after R, as is possible in OIr. orthography. The most likely analysis of MASOC is as corresponding to the OIr. adjective mosach ‘filthy, stinking; bristly’. This is used as a formula word, perhaps with the meaning ‘slave’, in I-KER-086 (= CIIC 216; Whitefield, Co. Kerry; see that entry for the etymology of the word). Another alleged occurrence in W-BRE-004 (= CIIC 327; Crick­howel, Breckonshire) is very uncertain. For this it has to be assumed that due to the absence of stress in the second and third syllable, the vowels of the underlying mosach had been reduced to schwa, which in turn had been written alternatingly as A and O. If this analysis is correct, the name has the non-complimentary meaning ‘having a filthy/stinking hole or eye’. Alterna­tively, -MASOC could be derived from the OIr. adjective mass ‘elegant’ (McManus 1991: 178). The name could then mean ‘having an elegant eye’, ‘having an elegant hole’, or, con­ceivably, ‘red and elegant’. The matter is complicated by the fact that DERCMASOC probably corresponds to the OIr. name Derc­mossach, which is once found as the name of a son of the mythical king Cathaír Már (CGH 585). In this OIr. form, the -o- of the second syllable must have been analogically rein­tro­duced into an earlier *Dercmasach from the simple adjective mosach. However, this seem­ingly obvious comparison is beset with problems. The spelling Dercmossach is found in the copies of the genea­logies in the Book of Leinster and in the Book of Ballymote, while in Raw­linson B 502 the name is spelt Dergmosach, and Derc­mais­each in the Book of Lecan. The spelling Dergmosach supports the analysis of the first element as derg ‘red’ with /g/. Influenced by this variant reading, and taking derg ‘red’ as an intensifier for ‘very’, Zieg­ler (1994: 166) in fact analyses the ogam name DERCMASOC as ‘very stinking’. However, Rawlinson B 502’s Derg­mosach could be a hyper­correct spelling for Dercmosach in its archetype, by a scribe who felt squeamish over the meaning of that name. Judging by his variant spelling Derc­maiseach, the scribe of the Book of Lecan, on the other hand, ap­pears to have understood the name to contain a second ele­ment derived from the OIr. adjec­tive mass ‘elegant’. Finally, it is conceivable that the ending -OC of DERCMASOC represents a rare instance of a ‘Britishism’, like in SONNOCC (I-KER-063 = CIIC 194A; Rathduff 2, Co. Kerry). -OC could represent the OIr. diminutive suffix -óc, which has been borrowed from Old British *-ɔ̄g < *-āko-.

The names in this inscription have already undergone lowering, apocope, syncope and various effects of the reduction of unstressed vowels and belong therefore to the late phase of PrimIr. At the same time, SEDANI and CAT­TABBOTT show unsyncopated forms with A probably standing for the reduced vowel schwa, as could be the case with -A- in DERC­MA­SOC. Furthermore, some spelling habits are reminis­cent of OIr. habits, even though no final certainty can be achieved in this regard. In total, the inscription belongs at least to the late 6th century, if not even the early 7th.
